author: Athanasius of Alexandria
book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: Introduction to de Decretis or Defence of the Nicene Definition.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: This letter must have been written in the interval between the return of Athanasius in 346 and his flight in 356.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: Acacius was already (§3) Bishop of Cæsarea 339; Eusebius of Nicomedia is not referred to as though still living (he died 342).

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: Moreover the language of §2 (“for in no long time they will turn to outrage,” &c.) implies a period of actual peace, but with a prospect of the repetition of the scenes of the year 339.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: This actually occurred in 356.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: Accordingly we must probably place the tract under the sole reign of Constantius, between 351 and the end of 355.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: It is written in answer to a friend who in disputing with Arians had been posed by their objection to the use of non-scriptural terms in the Nicene Definition.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: He accordingly asks for some account of what the council had done.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: Athanasius begins his answer by stigmatising the evasions and inconsistency of the Arianisers, and describing their conduct at the council, and how they eventually subscribed to the terms now complained of (1–5).

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: He then investigates the meaning of the divine Sonship (6–14), and how its true meaning is brought out by the other titles of the Son (15–17).

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: Coming to the non-scriptural expressions he shews how they were forced upon the council by the evasions of the Arians (18–20), and that they express no sense not to be found in Scripture (21–24).

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: Moreover, they had already been in use in the Church, as is shewn by extracts from Theognostus, the two Dionysii, and Origen (25–27).

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: Lastly (28–32) he discusses the term ἀγένητος, applied by the Arians (especially Asterius) to the Father, in contrast, not to the creation, but to the Son, who is thereby implied to be γένητος.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: He insists on ‘Father’ not ‘ἀγένητος’ as the divine title authorised by Scripture.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: Lastly he appends, in proof of what he states in §3, the letter of Eusebius to the people of Cæsarea, containing the creed of the council, which, for reasons there stated, we have inserted above, pp.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: The interest of the letter is principally threefold; first on account of its notice of the proceedings at Nicæa (cf. ad Afr.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: 5), one of the few primary sources of our knowledge of what took place there: secondly, on account of its fragments of early writers, especially the Dionysii, of whom more will be said in the introduction to the next tract.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: With regard to Theognostus, the quotations in this tract and in Serap. iv.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: 9 are important in view of the somewhat damaging accounts of his teaching in the few other writers (Gregory of Nyssa, Photius) who mention him.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: Thirdly, the term ἀγένητος demands attention.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: It is impossible to give its exact force in idiomatic English: the rendering ‘Ingenerate’ adopted by Newman is perhaps the most unfortunate one imaginable.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: ‘Uncreated,’ a possible substitute, is also open to objection, firstly, as not distinguishing the word from the derivatives of κτίζειν, ποιεῖν, δημιουργεῖν, secondly, as giving it a passive sense, which does not inherently attach to it.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: For lack of a better word, ‘Unoriginate’ may perhaps be adopted.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: ‘That which has not (or cannot) come to be,’ ‘that which is not the result of a process,’—is what the word strictly signifies’—‘das Ungewordene.’

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: It was therefore strictly applicable to the Son as well as to the Father.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: But throughout the earlier stages of the Arian controversy the question was embarrassed by the homophones γέννητος and ἀγέννητος, generate or begotten, and unbegotten.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: The confusion of thought due to the resemblance of sound is reflected in the confusion of readings in the mss.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: Athanasius himself (Orat. i.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: 56) perceives the distinctive sense of ἀγέννητος.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: In the present tract and in Orat. i.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: 30, he has ἀγένητος only in view, the idea of begetting being absent.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: Here (and cf. de Syn.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: 46, note 5) he is denying that the Father is alone ἀγένητος, uncreated or without a ‘becoming.’

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: Accordingly although the word γεννήθεντα was consecrated and safeguarded in the Creed of Nicæa (Begotten not made), and although the distinctness of the derivatives of the two verbs was felt by Athanasius, and pointed out by others (Epiph.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: 64, 8), the use of either group of words was avoided by Catholics as dangerous.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: A clear distinction of the words and of their respective applicability is made by John Damascene Fid.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: I. viii. (see Lightfoot, Ignat. vol.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
verses.text: 2, excursus on Eph. §7, Thilo, ubi supra, Introd. p.

verses.book: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
